summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Right elbow pain exacerbated by pottery making.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports moderate, persistent pain in the inside of their right elbow, which is worsened by activities such as pottery making. The patient also reports pain radiating down their arm with wrist flexion and moderate tenderness at the medial epicondyle with wrist flexion and resisted pronation of the right forearm. The patient reports fatigue for the past two months. He denies blood in stool. He reports difficulty taking medications twice daily and enjoys baking.

Findings
The clinician suspects cervical radiculopathy. Physical exam reveals tenderness at the medial epicondyle with wrist flexion.

Assessment
The patient presents with symptoms consistent with lateral epicondylitis.

Plan
Order a sling for the patient's right arm. Order six hundred milligrams of ibuprofen every six hours. Order an A1C test. Order a referral to diabetic counseling. Increase metformin dosage to 1000mg twice daily. Order a cervical MRI.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right elbow pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Teresa Sullivan is a pleasant 33-year-old female who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of right elbow pain. Her pain began 4 days ago, without any improvement. She denies any specific injury; however, she has recently increased her usage of a pottery wheel in creating a bowl for her son. She is currently unable to work with the pottery wheel secondary to pain. Her pain is in the medial aspect of her elbow. She notes it radiates medially down her arm as well. The patient rates her pain level as 7 out of 10 at its worst. She reports the pain wakes her at night if she rolls over. She denies ibuprofen or ice provide pain relief. The patient denies any previous elbow injuries.

The patient has a history of hypertension. She states she has been checking her blood pressures regularly. She adds that her blood pressures have been in the 110s to 130s range. She affirms she is taking lisinopril 10 mg daily and denies missing doses.

Regarding her diabetes type 2, she reports she sometimes forgets to take metformin 500 mg twice daily. At times, her blood sugar levels range from 150 to 170 mg/dL. She states that she has a sweet tooth and indulges in home baked goods. For exercise, she walks in the evening and in the morning. She walks 1 to 2 miles per day around her neighborhood.

The patient enjoys working with ceramics as well as baking cakes and cinnamon rolls for family gatherings.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient reports history of hypertension and diabetes type 2.

MEDICATIONS

Patient reports taking lisinopril 10 mg and metformin 500 mg twice daily.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right elbow pain.

VITALS

Blood Pressure: 120/70 mmHg.
Pulse Rate: Appropriate.
Respiratory Rate: 18 breaths per minute.
Oxygen Saturation: 100% on room air.
Body Temperature: 97.9 degrees F.
Pain Level: 7/10 at its worst.
Weight: 5 lb increase since last visit.

PHYSICAL EXAM

CV: No murmurs, gallops or rubs. Pulses are equal bilaterally.
RESPIRATORY: Normal respiratory effort, no respiratory distress. Lungs are clear bilaterally to auscultation.
SKIN: No rash or lesions.
MSK: Examination of the right elbow: Moderate tenderness at the medial epicondyle. Pain with resisted pronation of the right forearm. Pain with flexion and bending the right wrist. No bruising or swelling. No discoloration or lesions.

RESULTS

An x-ray of the right elbow was reviewed today. It demonstrated no evidence of any fractures. No other abnormalities are noted.

ASSESSMENT

1. Right elbow pain, consistent with medial epicondylitis.
2. Diabetes type 2.
3. Hypertension.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with her regarding her current symptoms. I have explained to her that her elbow pain is consistent with medial epicondylitis and was most likely caused by overuse and damage to the tendons in her arm. I have recommended that we treat the patient conservatively. The patient will wear a sling on her right arm while she is awake to provide increased support. I advised her to ice her right elbow for 20 minutes 3 times per day. She will take ibuprofen 600 mg every 6 hours with food for 1 week. I have also advised her to hold off on creating pottery for the next 2 weeks to allow her arm to heal. If the patient continues to experience persistent elbow pain, we may consider formal physical therapy at her next visit.

I have encouraged the patient to check and record her blood sugars every day. I will increase her metformin to 1000 mg twice a day. The patient and I discussed the importance of her taking her medication on a regular basis twice a day. I have also recommended that she be referred to diabetic counseling for more information on the steps she can take to manage her diabetes. I will also order a hemoglobin A1c test for a more accurate assessment of her long-term blood sugar levels.

The patient’s hypertension is currently controlled with medication and exercise. I have instructed her to continue taking lisinopril 10 mg. I also encouraged her to continue with her walking routine.

All questions were answered.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 2 weeks to review the results of the hemoglobin A1c test and discuss her progress.

Chief Complaint
Right elbow pain exacerbated by pottery making.

The patient reports moderate, persistent pain in the inside of their right elbow, which is worsened by activities such as pottery making. section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a diagnostic impression/synthesis by the clinician, not an objective exam or test result, and belongs in the Assessment section.

section_miscategorizations.text: Physical exam reveals tenderness at the medial epicondyle with wrist flexion.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Tenderness on exam is already described as a patient-reported symptom in HPI; here it is explicitly labeled as a physical exam finding and should be in Findings, not HPI. (Note: in the provided note, the same content appears in HPI as patient report and in Findings as exam; the exam phrasing is correctly in Findings, but the HPI version is miscategorized.)

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient also reports pain radiating down their arm with wrist flexion and moderate tenderness at the medial epicondyle with wrist flexion and resisted pronation of the right forearm.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This sentence describes specific provoked tenderness and pain with resisted maneuvers, which are characteristic of physical exam findings rather than typical patient-reported history, and could mislead a reader about what was actually examined.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports fatigue for the past two months.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Fatigue is a separate, chronic issue not clearly linked to the elbow complaint; it would be more clearly organized as an additional active problem in the Assessment, though its presence in HPI is not highly misleading.

This is a review-of-systems-type negative finding; while often documented with HPI in some styles, under this template it aligns better with objective/ROS findings than with the focused elbow pain history.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient presents with symptoms consistent with lateral epicondylitis.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The diagnostic label given (lateral epicondylitis) conflicts with the clearly medial-epicondyle-focused history and exam; while in the correct section type, the specific diagnosis text is clinically inconsistent and could mislead a reader about the problem being assessed.

Although formally a plan item, it is unrelated to the documented chief complaint and HPI (elbow pain) and appears to address a separate, undocumented problem (diabetes), which can mislead about the focus of this encounter under the given template semantics.

section_miscategorizations.text: Order a referral to diabetic counseling.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Similar to the A1C order, this is a plan for diabetes management without corresponding diabetes-related history, findings, or assessment in the appropriate sections, creating a mismatch that can confuse the reader about the visit's assessed problems.

section_miscategorizations.text: Increase metformin dosage to 1000mg twice daily.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a diabetes medication adjustment with no diabetes problem listed in Assessment or described in HPI/Findings; while structurally a plan item, its lack of linkage to an assessed problem makes its placement misleading in the context of this template.
